rulings.Case Title: RASHNI SINGH VS. ASHLEY DAVIS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants' Motion To Augment Expert Witness List
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 29, 2018, Line 7, Defendants' Motion To Augment Expert Witness List. Defendants Ashley and Philip Davis' motion to augment expert witness list to add Brian Doherty is denied. The premise of the motion is that defendants were surprised that plaintiff Rashni Singh's expert Dr. Lawrence Nordhoff testified at his deposition that he had opinions regarding biomechanics of the accident that is the subject of this case. However, a review of the papers filed by Ms. Singh in support of her ex parte application to substitute Dr. Nordhoff in place of her previously designated expert Dr. Winthrop Smith shows: 1) Ms. Singh's May 25, 2018 designation of Dr. Smith stated that Dr. Smith would provide opinions regarding "the forces, mechanics and dynamics of the subject collision"; 2) Dr. Nordhoff is an expert in "traffic accident reconstruction, biomechanics and injury causation analysis"; and 3) Dr. Nordhoff's curriculum vitae states his fields include "biomechanics." These papers establish that Ms. Singh had designated an expert in biomechanics more than three months ago and defendants were on notice that both Dr. Smith and Dr. Nordhoff were designated to testify about biomechanics of the accident that is the subject of this case. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Singh is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: WOODSIDE PRIORITY, LLC VS. ANDREA D. MCGARY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Andrea Mcgary'S Motion For Summary Judgment, Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication. (For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 29, 2018, Line 1, Defendant Andrea Mcgary'S Motion For Summary Judgment, Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication. (part 2 of 2) (For Tentative Ruling Only) In particular defendants did not show that Woodside could not prove its allegation that defendants misrepresented their skills to induce Woodside to incur attorney's fees, which Woodside incurred. Because defendants did not show that they are able to defeat the entirety of the intentional misrepresentation claim, they are not entitled to summary adjudication on that claim per CCP 437c(f)(1). Similarly, defendants have not shown that Woodside is unable to obtain punitive damages on its intentional misrepresentation claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which complies with CCP 437c(g) and repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.(part 2 of 2) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: WOODSIDE PRIORITY, LLC VS. ANDREA D. MCGARY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 29, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT ANDREA MCGARY's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication. (part 1 of 2) Defendants Andrea McGary and Daniel Iannitelli's motion for summary judgment on plaintiff Woodside Priority, LLC's first amended complaint is denied and its alternate motion for summary adjudication is: a) granted as to the first cause of action for professional negligence, the portion of the second cause of action for negligent misrepresentation, the third cause of action for breach of fiduciary duties and the fourth cause of action for breach of contract and b) denied as to the portion of the second cause of action for intentional misrepresentation and the request for punitive damages. Woodside did not oppose the motion as to the portion of its second cause of action for negligent misrepresentation and its third cause of action for breach of fiduciary duties. Woodside's professional negligence and breach of contract claims allege that defendants (1) failed to properly prosecute the fraud/nondischargeability adversary action in bankruptcy court and (2) failed to obtain a nondischargeability clause in Woodside's settlement with William Garlock. Both claims are based on the same allegations and are properly considered together. Woodside's attempt to include billing issues as a component of its breach of contract claim is not permissible on this motion as that theory of recovery is not alleged in the first amended complaint. "In a litigation malpractice action, the plaintiff must establish that but for the alleged negligence of the defendant attorney, the plaintiff would have obtained a more favorable judgment or settlement in the action in which the malpractice allegedly occurred." (Viner v. Sweet (2003) 30 Cal.4th 1232, 1241.) Defendants satisfy their summary judgment motion burden as to the contention that they failed to properly prosecute the adversary action. Defendants submitted evidence showing that: a) while Mr. Garlock may have been sloppy, there was no indication that he acted fraudulently (Garlock Depo. 25:24-26:6; 87:13-19); 2) Andrew Litton testified at the trial that the most important document that he relied on when agreeing to the Mitten transaction was the 12/31/07 financial statement (Trial Transcript 35:3-8); 3) Mr. Litton acknowledged at his deposition that he could not say whether he relied on that document when the funding occurred on 12/20/07 (Litton Depo. 272:21-273:6); 4) Mr. Litton further admitted that he could not say whether he received the financial statements before agreeing to the transaction (Litton Depo, 87:4-18); and 5) the impetus for the Mitten transaction was Mr. Litton's track record with Mr. Garlock and Mr. Litton's general understanding of Mr. Garlock's wealth based on Mr. Litton's historical access to Mr. Garlock's finances (Defendants' Facts 37-38). Woodside did not submit any evidence creating a triable dispute that the defendants should have been able to successfully prosecute the adversary action. There was no specific fraudulent representation that caused Mr. Litton to agree to the Mitten transaction. Woodside cannot prove malpractice or breach of contract based on the lack of a nondischargeability clause in the settlement agreement. The undisputed evidence shows that the settlement agreement was negotiated and signed during the course of a mediation. (Defendants' Facts 14-15.) Per Amis v. Greenberg Traurig LLP (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 331, 340, Woodside cannot rely on mediation communications to prove liability. Defendants did not satisfy their summary judgment motion burden as to the intentional misrepresentation claim and the request for punitive damages. Defendants did not sufficiently address all of Woodside's fraud theories or recoveries. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: AMANDA ANDERSON VS. MARIN GENERAL HOSPITAL ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 29, 2018, Line 6, DEFENDANT MARIN GENERAL HOSPITAL's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Defendant Marin General Hospital's demurrer to the fifth and seventh causes of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress and dependent adult abuse in the complaint filed by plaintiff Amanda Anderson is overruled as to both causes of action. For her IIED claim, Ms. Anderson adequately alleges that the Hospital's employees intentionally restrained her and performed a takedown, their actions were intentional, outrageous, malicious and done with the intent to cause harm or with reckless disregard that those actions would cause her to suffer humiliation, mental anguish, and emotional and physical distress, and as a result of the employees' conduct, she suffered emotional and physical distress. For her dependent adult abuse claim in violation of Welfare and Institutions Code 15657, Ms. Anderson pleads, with the required particularity, facts alleging that she was a dependent on the Hospital at the time of the incident, the Hospital's employees physically abused her and battered her by intentionally, improperly, and recklessly restraining her and performing a takedown, she did not consent to the touching, and as a result, she sustained injuries. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Anderson is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: AMANDA ANDERSON VS. MARIN GENERAL HOSPITAL ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 29, 2018, Line 6, DEFENDANT MARIN GENERAL HOSPITAL's MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT. Defendant Marin General Hospital's motion to strike portions of the complaint filed by plaintiff Amanda Anderson that seek punitive damages is denied. Welfare & Institutions Code 15657 allows Ms. Anderson to seek punitive damages for violation of the Elder Abuse and Dependent Adult Civil Protection Act. As stated in the order on the Hospital's demurrer, Ms. Anderson has adequately alleged a claim for violation of that Act. CCP 425.13 is not applicable to a claim for violation of that Act. (Covenant Care, Inc. v. Superior Court (2004) 32 Cal.4th 771, 783.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Anderson is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)
